original_abstract:
Maintaining high water quality is essential for human health, biodiversity, industrial stability, and the overall environment. However, increasing pollution levels in recent decades have necessitated more robust monitoring and predictive tools. This study addresses the challenge of accurately estimating the Water Quality Index (WQI)—a critical indicator of water safety—and the resulting Water Quality Classification (WQC). 

To achieve high predictive performance, we utilized various machine learning techniques enhanced by systematic parameter optimization and tuning via the grid search method. We developed two distinct sets of models: four classification algorithms—Random Forest (RF), Extreme Gradient Boosting (XGBoost), Gradient Boosting (GB), and Adaptive Boosting (AdaBoost)—to categorize water samples into quality classes (WQC); and four regression algorithms—K-Nearest Neighbor (KNN), Decision Tree (DT), Support Vector Regressor (SVR), and Multi-Layer Perceptron (MLP)—to predict numerical WQI values. The research employed a dataset containing seven key chemical and biological features across 1,991 instances, following a rigorous preprocessing phase that involved mean imputation for missing data and normalization.

Performance was evaluated using a comprehensive suite of metrics. Classification efficacy was measured through accuracy, recall, precision, F1 score, and Matthews’s Correlation Coefficient (MCC), while regression success was assessed using Mean Absolute Error (MAE), Median Absolute Error (MedAE), Mean Square Error (MSE), and the coefficient of determination (R²). The experimental results demonstrate that the tuned Gradient Boosting (GB) model achieved the highest classification accuracy at 99.50%. For numerical prediction, the MLP regressor significantly outperformed the other models, reaching an R² value of 99.8%. These findings suggest that optimized machine learning frameworks provide a highly reliable approach for real-time water quality assessment and resource management.
humanized_abstract:
Water quality is a very important factor for the protection of human health, biodiversity, industry and the environment. The increasing trend in water pollution over the last several decades has made it crucial to have a sophisticated water quality monitoring and forecasting system. In this study the challenges faced in estimating the Water Quality Index (WQI) of the water bodies and consequently, the Water Quality Classification (WQC) has been examined.

Four classification models such as Random Forest (RF), Extreme Gradient Boosting (XGBoost), Gradient Boosting (GB) and AdaBoost and four regression models such as K-Nearest Neighbor (KNN), Decision Tree (DT), Support Vector Regressor (SVR) and Multi-Layer Perceptron (MLP) are built to evaluate their performances in identifying the quality classes of water (WQC) and to calculate the water quality index (WQI) values. The suggested models are implemented in Python language by considering the impact of various machine learning techniques that achieve high accuracy performance after optimizing their parameters by applying the grid search method. For building the models, the dataset is constituted of 7 key chemical and biological characteristics for 1991 samples after applying mean imputation and normalization techniques for missing values.

The performance of different models was evaluated with a set of metrics. The accuracy of the classification of water quality samples was verified with the following measures: accuracy, recall, precision, F1 score, and Matthews’s Correlation Coefficient (MCC). In addition, Mean Absolute Error (MAE), Median Absolute Error (MedAE), Mean Square Error (MSE), and the coefficient of determination (R²) were evaluated as measures of the success of the regression. Figure 6 shows that the tuned Gradient Boosting (GB) classifier provided the highest accuracy value among all models for water classification, reaching 99.50%. In this case, the MLP regressor showed a substantially higher value of R² in relation to the other regressors, reaching 99.8%. Overall, based on the high performance achieved with the optimized models, the application of machine learning approaches to achieve real time water quality monitoring and efficient water resource planning has been verified.
